ABSTRACT
Autonomous Vision-Based Robotic Medicine Delivery with Patient
Identification for Contagious Hospital Wards is an assistive robotic system
intended to reduce direct human contact during medicine handling while
ensuring the correct medicine is delivered to the intended patient or delivery
point. The system comprises two main components: a vision unit running on
a Raspberry Pi 5 with a camera module, and a six-axis robotic arm driven by
a PCA9685 servo controller. The camera captures the workspace, and a

point. The system comprises two main components: a vision unit running on
a Raspberry Pi 5 with a camera module, and a six-axis robotic arm driven by
a PCA9685 servo controller. The camera captures the workspace, and a
YOLO-based object detection model processes each frame to identify one of
the 11 medicine classes in the project dataset. When a valid medicine packet
and a patient identification cue (visual marker) are detected, the Raspberry Pi
sends a command to the servo firmware. The firmware stores smooth pose
sequences for the waist, shoulder, elbow, wrist pitch, wrist roll, and gripper

and a patient identification cue (visual marker) are detected, the Raspberry Pi
sends a command to the servo firmware. The firmware stores smooth pose
sequences for the waist, shoulder, elbow, wrist pitch, wrist roll, and gripper
joints, and executes the pick-and-place delivery. The vision code supports
image, folder, video, USB camera, and Pi camera inputs. The prototype
demonstration shows the robot operating on a desktop setup with a live camera
feed, approaching the medicine packet, aligning the gripper, and completing
the delivery sequence in a controlled, repeatable manner. Experimental

demonstration shows the robot operating on a desktop setup with a live camera
feed, approaching the medicine packet, aligning the gripper, and completing
the delivery sequence in a controlled, repeatable manner. Experimental
evaluation demonstrated robust recognition performance, where the
normalized confusion matrix produced diagonal values ranging from 0.89 to
0.99, indicating strong class-level prediction consistency, and the system
achieved a mean class-wise recognition accuracy of 96.5%, confirming the
effectiveness and reliability of the proposed medicine identification and

achieved a mean class-wise recognition accuracy of 96.5%, confirming the
effectiveness and reliability of the proposed medicine identification and
delivery framework.

INTRODUCTION
1.1 Overview
The system is designed as a vision-guided robotic assistant rather than as a
fully autonomous mobile robot. A camera observes the workspace, a trained
object detector identifies the medicine packet and the intended patient or target

The system is designed as a vision-guided robotic assistant rather than as a
fully autonomous mobile robot. A camera observes the workspace, a trained
object detector identifies the medicine packet and the intended patient or target
marker, and a servo-driven arm performs the physical pick-and-place action.
The project is built around a Raspberry Pi 5 for high-level processing, a
camera module for image acquisition, a PCA9685 servo driver for multi-
channel PWM generation, and a six-joint arm made with MG996R and MG90
servos.

camera module for image acquisition, a PCA9685 servo driver for multi-
channel PWM generation, and a six-joint arm made with MG996R and MG90
servos.
The motivation for choosing a fixed-location robotic assistant over a fully
autonomous mobile robot stems from the specific constraints of contagious
hospital wards. In these environments, the primary source of infection risk is
not long-distance navigation but rather the repeated, close-proximity
interactions between healthcare workers and patients during medicine

hospital wards. In these environments, the primary source of infection risk is
not long-distance navigation but rather the repeated, close-proximity
interactions between healthcare workers and patients during medicine
handovers. By focusing on the pick-and-place operation at a dedicated bedside
station, the system addresses the highest-frequency contact point while
avoiding the complexities of corridor navigation, door opening, and elevator
management. This design choice also simplifies regulatory considerations, as
a stationary robotic arm operating within a clearly defined workspace presents

avoiding the complexities of corridor navigation, door opening, and elevator
management. This design choice also simplifies regulatory considerations, as
a stationary robotic arm operating within a clearly defined workspace presents
fewer safety hazards than a mobile robot moving through uncontrolled
hospital corridors. The prototype therefore represents a pragmatic first step:
automating the most repetitive and contact-intensive part of the medicine
delivery chain while leaving navigation-related challenges for future
iterations.

automating the most repetitive and contact-intensive part of the medicine
delivery chain while leaving navigation-related challenges for future
iterations.
Although the project title uses the phrase patient identification, the
practical implementation in this report is a recipient-cue workflow based on a
visible marker, known visual cue, or equivalent tag. In this prototype, patient
identification means linking the detected patient cue to the correct medicine
delivery action.

1.2 Problem Statement
The central problem addressed by this project is not simply that medicine
delivery is repetitive; it is that repetitive delivery becomes risky and inefficient
when it takes place in contagious wards. Every unnecessary room entry adds

The central problem addressed by this project is not simply that medicine
delivery is repetitive; it is that repetitive delivery becomes risky and inefficient
when it takes place in contagious wards. Every unnecessary room entry adds
to the infection-control burden, increases the amount of protective equipment
that must be used, and exposes healthcare staff to a workflow that is physically
tiring and emotionally demanding. A robot that can handle a simple delivery
action from a fixed location does not solve every hospital problem, but it does
remove one recurring source of contact and delay.

tiring and emotionally demanding. A robot that can handle a simple delivery
action from a fixed location does not solve every hospital problem, but it does
remove one recurring source of contact and delay.
The problem is also technical. Most student-built robotic systems are either
vision-focused or motion-focused, but not both in a tightly integrated way. A
camera can detect an object, and a servo arm can move to a pose, yet the
meaningful challenge is connecting those two abilities in a way that is reliable
enough to demonstrate in front of examiners. This is why the project uses a

camera can detect an object, and a servo arm can move to a pose, yet the
meaningful challenge is connecting those two abilities in a way that is reliable
enough to demonstrate in front of examiners. This is why the project uses a
deterministic sequence rather than a vague autonomous promise. The goal is
to prove that the sensing and actuation loop can be made stable enough to
repeat under real indoor conditions.

correct packet, walking to the patient room, and handing over the medicine
becomes disproportionately taxing when each such cycle requires donning and

The scope of the project is deliberately narrow enough to be practical and
broad enough to be meaningful. It includes a visual recognition pipeline, a
robotic arm control layer, a communication protocol, and a demonstration

robotic arm control layer, a communication protocol, and a demonstration
workflow that links all of them together. The project does not attempt to solve
path planning in a hospital corridor, full patient management, or autonomous
navigation across multiple rooms. Those functions are valuable, but they
belong to later development stages.

applied to medicine recognition tasks. Studies have trained YOLOv5,
YOLOv7, and YOLOv8 on custom datasets containing various medicine
packaging types, achieving mAP@0.5 scores above 0.90. These systems are
attractive because they offer real-time inference on edge devices such as the

• Serial (RS-232 / USB): Simple and reliable, used for low-bandwidth
command transmission. The present project uses serial over Bluetooth.

• ROS (Robot Operating System): A flexible framework for publishing

• ROS (Robot Operating System): A flexible framework for publishing
and subscribing to topics across distributed nodes. ROS is powerful but
has a steep learning curve and may be overkill for a compact prototype.
• MQTT / WebSockets: Used for cloud-connected robots, but
introduces latency and dependency on network infrastructure.

initializes the PCA9685 channels and moves the joints to a safe home state.

current for simultaneous servo movement. Servo motors draw the most current
when they start moving or when they hold load at a non-neutral angle, so the
power arrangement must tolerate short current peaks. If those peaks are not
handled properly, the voltage rail can drop and the entire prototype may appear
unstable even though the software is correct. For that reason, the power
discussion is not a minor detail but a central part of the hardware design.

handled properly, the voltage rail can drop and the entire prototype may appear
unstable even though the software is correct. For that reason, the power
discussion is not a minor detail but a central part of the hardware design.
The logic board and the servo rail are treated as distinct consumers. The
Raspberry Pi and the camera need clean logic power, while the servo bank
needs a stronger current source. The purpose of this separation is to keep the
high-load actuators from disturbing the computing side. That design habit is
common in embedded systems because it improves repeatability and makes

common in embedded systems because it improves repeatability and makes
faults easier to isolate. If the arm behaves oddly, the engineer can inspect the
servo rail first instead of guessing whether the problem is in the model.
